data.title: The Real Price Of Oil Is Not What You Think
data.description:
In less than 48 hours the ceasefire will expire, and the U.S.–Iran war might enter a new phase of escalation. The U.S. wants to start a new round of negotiations today, but Iran doesn’t seem willing to accept. According to Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei, the U.S. “violated the ceasefire from the beginning of its implementation,” starting with the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and closing with the overnight capture of the Iranian container ship. On the other hand, President Trump has also claimed that Iran violated the ceasefire by firing bullets at French and British ships in the Strait of Hormuz. The latter remains closed/highly restricted 52 days after the start of the war.

The Tuesday evening deadline is approaching, and both parties seem more distant than a couple of days ago. As a result, at the time of writing, crude oil WTI (CL1:COM) / Brent (CO1:COM) prices are up +6% while main U.S. stock market futures are down ~ 0.50%.

Why Should You Pay Attention To Physical Oil Prices?

With the current 6% increase, the Brent Oil price is ~ $95 per barrel, which is pretty high but at least not in triple digits. However, oil prices can vary greatly for multiple reasons, from the location in which it is extracted to the type of contract we are considering. The oil prices you see on popular websites like Seeking Alpha or TradingView are primarily Continuous Spot CFDs, basically future contracts whose expiry is the closest possible. These are the prices that set the baseline for oil prices and are used by traders who want to speculate on price fluctuations. These transactions are cash-settled, which means that you are speculating on the oil prices, but you are not actually buying the underlying barrels of oil.

On the other hand, if you are a refinery, you need the underlying barrels of oil for your operation, not a cash-settled transaction. However, the price you are going to pay is not the same, as you need to add the shipping & physical surcharges.

Typically, the physical oil (red line) tends to be a little higher than the paper oil (green line), but the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has created an unprecedented breakdown in the global energy supply chain.

While the whole market is focused on the paper oil (~$95 per barrel), the real threat for the economy lies in the physical oil (~$112 per barrel). This is the actual price that refineries are paying to run their operations (and it will probably be passed on to consumers), and despite the sharp drop, it is still 80% higher than the beginning of January. At the end of March, the physical oil price matched the 2022 highs, and it currently shows a massive $17 premium over the paper oil. What does this mean?

It means that companies that need physical oil don’t follow the same logic as those who just trade oil for speculative/hedging reasons. They are willing to pay a massive premium to keep running their activity, so it is a constant competition against peers for every single drop of oil. Traders don’t need the physical delivery, and they simply speculate on their expectations. Since the war is expected to end anytime soon, this explains why paper oil prices are much lower than physical oil.

However, I believe that between the two, physical oil is what investors should prioritize. Physical oil is a better indicator of the current state of the energy crisis, and $112 per barrel of oil is way too high to not expect a significant upward pressure in the global inflation rate over the next months. Four days ago, the Eurostat issued the inflation report for the month of March, and it doesn’t bode well.

The euro area annual inflation rate was 2.60% in March, way higher than the 1.90% of February. Needless to say, the most volatile component was energy, from -0.30% pp in February to +0.48% pp in March. We are approaching the end of April, and the physical oil price remains in triple digits, so I have lots of doubts that ECB headline inflation expectations are going to be met. The ECB staff has forecasted a 2.60% inflation rate in 2026; therefore, even though we are in the middle of an energy crisis, it shouldn’t increase from now on.

In my opinion, the EU won’t escape a higher inflation rate, and of course, the same reasoning applies to all the other major economies, including the U.S. Even though the market is pricing in lower physical oil prices in the second part of the year, both the July delivery (blue line) and December delivery (orange line) are above ~ $95 per barrel, ~ 60% higher than four months ago.

The energy supply shock is bound to haunt us for the entire 2026 (even if the war ends today), and it might even extend to the beginning of 2027 as well. Historically, energy crises don’t end in less than 2 months.

The Duration Of The Conflict Is The Key

The major negotiating leverage at Iran’s disposal is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, so it is reasonable to believe that it will remain closed as long as a final agreement is reached. Besides this, there isn’t much that Iran can do to really harm the U.S. and the world economy. However, even though weaker from a military perspective, Iran has a significant advantage: it doesn’t need to rush to reach an agreement. After 47 years of disputes, reaching an agreement over the next months would be considered a success. But this kind of timing is definitely not good for the U.S. Imagine a scenario in which the Strait of Hormuz remains closed even during the summer session: physical oil prices would challenge previous highs, if not more.

Early last week, we had multiple pieces of positive news that made us believe that the war was almost over, especially the Israel–Lebanon 10-day ceasefire. Furthermore, a new round of negotiations between the U.S. and Iran was expected before the ceasefire deadline (Tuesday evening) in Islamabad, avoiding a new phase of escalation. However, things changed pretty fast over the weekend: Israel didn’t stop attacking Lebanon despite the ceasefire, and Iran, contrary to the U.S., stated that it has no plans to send negotiators to Islamabad. Why? According to a Foreign Ministry spokesman, the U.S. has violated the ceasefire multiple times, and it is ready to strike back at Israel and the U.S. if they start new attacks. Of course, President Trump is not appreciating at all the Iranian unwillingness to find an agreement:

We’re offering a very fair and reasonable DEAL, and I hope they take it because, if they don’t, the United States is going to knock out every single Power Plant, and every single Bridge, in Iran. NO MORE MR. NICE GUY! They’ll come down fast, they’ll come down easy and, if they don’t take the DEAL, it will be my Honor to do what has to be done, which should have been done to Iran, by other Presidents, for the last 47 years. IT’S TIME FOR THE IRAN KILLING MACHINE TO END!

President DONALD J. TRUMP.

Basically, 52 days have passed since the start of the war, and we are back to square one. The U.S. and Iran still have very different opinions, but what investors care about the most is that the Strait of Hormuz is still closed. The longer it stays closed, the more likely the stagflation scenario gets. As of now, the stock market is totally downplaying the likelihood of a stagflationary period; in fact, it has recently set a new all-time high.

From a purely technical perspective, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) is close to beating a long-term trend started during the pandemic. If it gets above the resistance, we are directed toward uncharted territory; if it goes down, the worst-case seems to be 6,000 for now. To get to this point, I believe the war should restart on Wednesday, or at least, the economy should experience higher-than-expected inflation rates over the next weeks or a weak earnings season. With such high energy prices, investors have a lot of questions on the ROI of the billions of dollars that AI hyperscalers are going to invest.

data.title: Moog: This Quiet Defense Winner Still Has Upside
data.description:
Moog has gained 6.9% since my last report outperforming the S&P 500’s 3% gain. That is not a huge outperformance but still more than twice the index return. I believe that is actually quite remarkable given that aerospace and defense stocks have not been favored by the market since the war in Iran started. In this report, I discuss the opportunities that Moog has, the upcoming earnings expectations and present my increased price target supporting my buy rating.

Moog Could Have Elevated Opportunities Amid Rising Tensions

What we have seen is increased demand for missile and missile defense. While contracts may be awarded, I don’t view increased missile defense as a substantial near-term driver. The reason for that is that defense prime contractors are already executing on previously established frameworks to increase production and additional demand would more often than not be either for even higher production in years from now or extending peak production levels. So, near-term opportunities may be limited. There may be some funds flowing into the supply chain to start expanding the capacity beyond previously established levels to support replenishing the missile stockpiles, but I do not expect those to be big dollars at this stage.

Moog supplies motion control, actuation and control systems that are used in missiles for guidance and propulsion control and fighter jets such as the F-35 for flight controls. The company’s products are also used in turrets and satellite systems. With the eye on the war in Iran, the drivers would be replenishment demand for missiles and higher maintenance and spare parts for air operations. Moog is a supplier to the defense primes and with a potentially prolonged conflict, we could see some sort of supply chain lock in which could give Moog good backlog and revenue visibility and strong pricing power as the war environment pivots price sensitivity to delivery-over-cost. Since Moog provides products that cannot be easily replaced by other suppliers, it has superior pricing power without bidding risks since it is simply part of an integrated supply chain of the defense primes. In case of a ground war, Moog could even see stronger demand from ground systems and additional air operations demanding more maintenance and spares.

Part of all this is, however, offset by potential risks of contract costs inflating and that is also why aerospace and defense stocks declined when the war in Iran started.

Moog Expected To Show Double Digit Sales Growth With EPS Growth In The Twenties

Moog will be reporting its Q2 2025 earnings on April 24. Wall Street analysts are expecting the company to report 10.5% year-on-year growth likely driven by strength in commercial aviation, defense and space markets. Over the past month, the estimates increased by 1.5% and by 0.5% and 2.9% over the past three and six months.

EPS estimates point to an even stronger increase with 23.1% growth to $2.36 with no change over the past month and upward revisions of 2.6% and 2.75% over the past six and three months. What will be interesting is any comment from management on tariffs. The Supreme Court of the United States ruled that tariffs imposed under IEEPA were illegal, but the administration replaced those with new tariffs using different parts of the law and tariffs under Section 301 and Section 232 remain.

Moog Remains Attractive As Growth Drivers Better Align

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Moog, the expectations have not substantially changed with a 0.6% downward revision on EBITDA and free cash flow for 2026-2028. I have been covering Moog since May 2023 and since then the stock has returned over 200% showing a roughly 3:1 performance against the S&P 500. Moog’s problem back then was that it had cost overruns at some of its space programs and the company had a 2.4x net debt leverage. While that was not awful, there was a need for Moog to increase its free cash flows to better carry debt maturities. For FY26, the expectation is that the company would generate nearly $200 million in free cash flow compared to a $37 million free cash flow burn in FY23. The company has substantially improved and while they still need to refinance some debt as they did earlier this year to streamline the debt maturities, their ability to repay debt from free cash flow is improving in the years ahead and the net debt leverage has improved substantially, which also enables share repurchases.

The company is expected to grow revenues by 8% annually and I am currently expecting EBITDA growth slightly below that driven by some delays in commercial aerospace production ramp ups and lower margins in the early phase of new military programs, but the free cash flow is expected to grow by 42% annually.

To value Moog, I am using the average of the company’s median EV/EBITDA implied price target of $227.52 and the peer group valuation of $505. This brings the price target to $366.26 against my prior price target of $349 and indicates 13.5% upside. One thing that we need to keep in mind is the current volatility in aerospace and defense stocks, which may provide some near-term noise in the share price performance.

For Moog, I am expecting margins of around 15% with clean free cash flow growth as capital expenditures are expected to be around $150-$160 million. With the improving cash flows that should lead to capital intensity tapering to 30% from 127% of operating cash flow years ago. Even with returning all free cash flow to shareholders, we see that the company’s net debt leverage is set to drop to 1.5x by FY28 and heading into the final years of this decade and into the next, the company should be able to see more robust growth from commercial airplane programs running at full capacity all at the same time.

Risk On Israel Shipments For Moog

One risk for Moog are the shipments to Israel. Moog's UK subsidiary sent a shipment bound for Israel through Belgium and those shipments have been seized due to missing declarations. Moog is providing actuators for airplanes used to train F-35 pilots for Israel as well as actuators for fire control systems and spare parts. The problem seems to be that Moog changed its shipping from FedEx (FDX) to UPS (UPS) and the carrier should normally apply for a transit license. So, this potentially could be a risk for Moog and/or UPS.

Conclusion: Moog Looks Overvalued But I Believe It is Not

Moog may look overvalued when we consider the company’s median EV/EBITDA. However, we note that the company has been working its way through a very challenging operational performance and debt load and it has been doing so rather well and that should provide support for the company to trade more in line with peers eventually. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating for Moog.

data.title: SCHD: A Decent Buy But Not To Beat The Market
data.description:
Performance Assessment

The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) initially lagged the S&P500 (SPY) since my last update on the stock, but has since staged a period of sharp outperformance, particularly YTD. Overall, it has slightly beaten the broad market index on a total shareholder return basis:

I have not switched from my 'Sell/Underperform' view in time.

Exposure Analysis of SCHD vs SPY

I don't like to view a broad market exposure ETF like SCHD in isolation. I find it more useful to assess it compared to a market index such as the S&P500 (SPY). So here's the exposure analysis of SCHD vs SPY across sectors and key industries:

For some of the major net exposure differences, I have highlighted favorable net exposures for SCHD in green, unfavorable net exposures for SCHD in red and a neutral exposures preference in yellow. I will discuss these points further in my thesis:

Elevator Pitch

  1. Weak consumer sentiment is a headwind for SCHD's consumer-weighted portfolio
  2. A sharp decline in oil prices could weigh down on SCHD's upstream O&G exposures
  3. GPU availability near troughs implies upside for semiconductors and SCHD is underexposed
  4. A sugar surplus can help margins in SCHD's overweight beverages exposure
  5. Growing manufacturing PMIs bodes well for SCHD's industrial sector overweight
  6. SCHD valuations are fair
  7. SCHD is in a classic uptrend

Weak Consumer Sentiment is a Headwind for SCHD's Consumer-Weighted Portfolio

The University of Michigan's (UoM) Consumer Sentiment index is known to be a reliable leading economic indicator espoused by renowned economists such as Bernard Baumohl who is the chief global economist at The Economic Outlook Group. If you want to know more about it, Baumohl has discussed this at length in his book, The Secrets of Economic Indicators.

The reading on the UoM Consumer Sentiment index looks really weak right now at all-time lows that go back more than 6 decades:

I believe this presents a demand headwind for sectors such as Consumer Discretionary and also Consumer Staples as people look to rationalize their spending amid constrained disposable income budgets.

Now, SCHD has a 14.3% net overweight exposure to Consumer Staples vs SPY but a 3.3% net underweight exposure to Consumer Discretionary vs SPY. So on balance, I view this as a headwind for SCHD.

Another interesting thing I want to highlight is SCHD's net 8.3% underweight to Interactive Media and Services. Companies such as Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) are in this category and the defining characteristic is that a lot of their revenues comes from ads. Typically, I would expect a weak consumer environment to translate to a weak ads environment. But in this case, I believe some of these headwinds are offset by improved, AI-driven ad decisions, which improves overall conversion rates and ads performance. That's why I have marked the underweight exposure to this industry as a neutral point for SCHD.

A Sharp Decline in Oil Prices Could Weigh Down on SCHD's Upstream O&G Exposures

SCHD has a big 11.7% net overweight to the Energy sector vs SPY. This includes a 6.7% net overweight to the upstream O&G sector that benefits from rising oil prices.

The problem is that according to market research from the US Energy Information Administration, oil prices do not have a lot of sustainable upside potential. In fact, prices are expected to fall about 30% back down to ~$60/bbl over the next 2 years:

This is because production from offline plants are expected to gradually come back online, which I suspect is driven by a need to fulfill the gaps from disruptions to the US-Iran supply chains.

GPU Availability Near Troughs Implies Upside for Semiconductors and SCHD is Underexposed

The chart below highlights how GPU availability has collapsed to the lowest levels since 2024:

Note that a GPU availability shortage does not just affect the GPU makers such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). It has domino effects across the entire semiconductors value chain if you consider the second and third-order effects. For example, Nvidia will look to book more leading-edge wafer capacity to expand its own production. This benefits foundry players such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). High, unmet demand for GPUs can also mean high, unmet demand for GPU component players in the memory, power, networking and even analog semiconductor segments.

Now, the last time GPU availability was so low, the semiconductor sector as represented by the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) outperformed the S&P500 massively. I believe similar conditions can lead to a semiconductors' outperformance right now:

This is not ideal for SCHD as it is woefully underexposed with a 5.8% underweight to the semiconductors industry and a 7.3% underweight to the Technology Hardware, Storage and Peripherals industry, to which many second-order tailwinds flow.

A Sugar Surplus Can Help Margins in SCHD's Overweight Beverages Exposure

SCHD's top overweight vs SPY is in the Soft Drinks and Non-alcoholic Beverages sector due to 4.0% and 3.9% weights in The Coca Cola Company (KO) and PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) respectively. As both these companies are growing revenues slowly at <5%, I view the margin drivers as being quite material for these companies' future outlook.

And from this perspective, sugar is a key input for PepsiCo and Coca Cola. And I note a favorable tailwind here as the sugar market is expected to be in a surplus for 2026 and 2027:

So I expect lower sugar costs to be a positive margin driver for these beverage companies, especially over the medium term. And SCHD is well-poised to reap the rewards of this exposure.

Growing Manufacturing PMIs Bodes Well for SCHD's Industrial Sector Overweight

The US Manufacturing PMIs have been printing a growing trend over the past 3 months. It is also encouraging to see that the rate of growth is accelerating. Growing new orders and contracting inventories are also good to see as this tends to be a leading indicator of growth in the manufacturing sector:

Overall, I think this bodes well for SCHD's small 2.7% net overweight to the Industrial sector.

SCHD valuations are fair

At a trailing P/E of 20.1x, SCHD is trading at a small discount to SPY's 21.6x and a large 38% discount to QQQ's 32.5x:

I find these valuations to be reasonable to mildly attractive.

SCHD is in a classic uptrend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

SCHD Technical Analysis

SCHD has been extremely resilient this year, selling off only 2.61% last month compared to the S&P500's 5.76% drawdown since the end of Feb'26. The technicals look clearly bullish as the prices have broken out strongly after a big re-accumulation zone on the monthly charts. From the chart momentum perspective, I see no good reason to not expect the uptrend to continue.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Relative to the SPY, I have a bearish outlook on SCHD as I find more unfavorable net exposures than favorable ones. But if we consider the absolute outlook for SCHD, I have a neutral view.
  • Valuation: Bullish due to valuation discounts vs the broader market indices.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish due to a clean uptrend setup.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: For an investor looking to outperform the S&P500, I have a bearish outlook on SCHD. I used to assign ratings purely based on relative performance but for simplicity's sake, I have reverted to expressing my view based on absolute performance. And from this perspective, the arguments tip in favor of a bullish view. So I rate it a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SMDV: Small-Cap Dividend Exposure With Lagging Total Returns
data.description:
The ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV) is an exchange-traded fund that aims to track the Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index. While the fund has returned 21.00% and 31.38% on a total returns basis over one-year and three-year periods, SMDV has underperformed when compared to its peers. Additionally, while SMDV previously had a history of dividend growth, the fund has recently failed to continue this trend, with a current TTM dividend growth rate of -8.54% (zero consecutive years of growth).

My investment thesis is the following: While SMDV provides exposure to small-cap companies with consistent dividend growth histories, its defensive positioning has limited its ability to outperform in the current market environment. The combination of underperformance and declining trailing-twelve-month dividend growth reduces its appeal on both an income and total return basis. Absent a more meaningful shift toward a risk-off environment that favors SMDV’s defensive positioning or a reacceleration of dividend growth, SMDV lacks any compelling near-term catalysts to warrant new capital allocation. For these reasons, I currently assign SMDV a “Hold” rating. Unless a defensive rotation were to materialize or dividend growth were to return to positive, SMDV is likely to continue to underperform its peers.

Fund Overview

The ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF first came to market in February 2015, giving us a little more than a decade of performance and fund management to consider.

As outlined in the fund’s summary prospectus, SMDV is a passively managed index fund, with the goal of tracking the performance of the Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index. The index is reevaluated annually in June, and weightings reset to equal weight each quarter in March, June, September, and December.

According to Seeking Alpha, the fund’s peers consist of other ETFs such as the T Rowe Price Dividend Growth ETF (TDVG), Capital Group Dividend Growers ETF (CGDG), iShares Russell 2000 Growth ETF (IWO), WisdomTree US SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DES), and the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL).

Index and Investment Process

As a starting point, the Russell 2000 Index is designed to measure the performance of small-cap equities in the United States. From there, the Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index targets a minimum of 40 companies that have shown 10+ years of dividend growth. Each of these holdings are equally weighted, given that no more than 30% is allocated to any one given sector. If fewer than 40 companies meet this criterion, companies with shorter dividend growth histories may be included in the index. The Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index is constructed and maintained by FTSE International Limited.

According to SMDV’s website, “under normal circumstances, [the fund] will invest at least 80% of its total assets in components of the Index or in instruments with similar economic characteristics.” With that said, the fund aims to hold all (or nearly all) index constituents, using equal weighting as dictated by the index. Fund rebalancings occur to match changes in the underlying index. Currently, the fund has 105 holdings.

Holdings & Performance

Over a three-year period, SMDV has underperformed compared to its peers, with a three-year total return of 31.38%. During this period, funds like CGDG, TDVG, and IWO all posted total returns exceeding 50%.

This disparity in performance can largely be attributed to the varying underlying methodologies and indices of the various funds, resulting in vastly different holdings. SMDV is largely concentrated in the financial sector (32.85%), with industrials and utilities also making up a significant portion of the portfolio (20.24% and 17.49%). Technology accounts for only 2.11% of holdings. In comparison, top-performing funds like CGDG maintain greater allocation percentages to technology as well as healthcare.

For the purpose of this article, I believe that the WisdomTree US SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DES) serves as a worthy alternative to SMDV for a cross-fund comparative analysis.

Both funds offer income-oriented investors exposure to US dividend-paying small caps; however, index construction and holdings differ. Where SMDV aims to track the returns of the Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index, DES aims to track the returns of the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend Index. More information on the index can be found here.

While both funds are concentrated towards financials (40.05% for SMDV and 34.71% for DES), SMDV maintains greater concentrations in utilities and producer manufacturing (17.61% and 14.29%), whereas DES has greater exposure to retail trade, consumer services, and technology services.

As a result, while trailing-twelve-month dividend yields are similar (2.41% compared to 2.42%), total return profiles differ. DES has provided total returns of 33.43% over a one-year period and 46.90% over a three-year period, outperforming SMDV by approximately 12 percentage points and 16 percentage points, respectively.

Peer Comparison

The two funds also differ in terms of expense ratios and dividend growth rates, with DES providing a marginally better expense ratio of 0.38% compared to 0.40%. Both funds have had negative dividend growth (-8.54% for SMDV and -5.92% for DES). As a result, SMDV has failed to maintain its previous four-year streak of YoY dividend growth.

On a comparative basis, DES also maintains marginally more attractive valuation metrics. Price-to-earnings, price-to-book, price-to-sales, and price-to-cash flow are all moderately better than those of SMDV. For investors seeking small-cap dividend exposure in the present environment, DES appears to currently be the better option.

Possible Future Scenarios

Going forward, SMDV’s relative performance will likely be driven less by stock-specific factors and more by macro regime shifts and factor rotation. As such, in my view, investors should view SMDV as a tactical allocation or add-on in a broader dividend-focused portfolio. Given its low beta of 0.82, SMDV can help reduce overall portfolio volatility, making it more appropriate as a defensive add-on rather than a core allocation within a dividend-focused strategy.

In a bullish scenario, market sentiment would need to shift more towards a risk-off/defensive positioning. Interest rates would remain somewhat elevated and decline slowly, supporting financials margins and stability. Investors would favor profitability and balance sheet strength rather than explosive growth and multiple expansion. In this scenario, I could see SMDV’s concentration in financials (and to a lesser extent, utilities and industrials) benefiting, providing total returns that outpace more growth-focused funds. In general, in a risk-off environment, SMDV becomes more attractive as a defensive small-cap allocation and could outperform other funds like DES.

In a bearish scenario, SMDV is likely to continue its underperformance if markets remain tech/AI-growth focused. In such cases, technology and higher-beta equities would continue to outperform. In my opinion, in such a scenario, the WisdomTree US SmallCap Dividend Fund would likely be a better allocation than SMDV.

My current positioning falls somewhere between the bullish and bearish scenarios. While I think underperformance on a comparative basis is still likely, I acknowledge that SMDV may fit into the portfolio of certain investors. Mainly, given the low beta and specific focus on US small-caps, SMDV can serve as a necessary income-generating way for investors to diversify within a broader dividend portfolio.

If dividend growth were to return to positive, and/or if financials start to outperform on a comparative basis, I could see myself upgrading SMDV from a “Hold” to a “Buy”. Likewise, if underperformance were to continue/worsen, or if dividend growth were to materially deteriorate, I think a future downgrade would be warranted.

Risks to Underlying Thesis

The main risk that I see to my thesis is a shift in market sentiment toward more defensive positioning. For current holders of SMDV, this would not be a bad thing, as it would likely benefit SMDV’s portfolio construction. While the current environment has favored broader/more diversified dividend strategies such as CGDG, and funds like SCHD and FDVV [considered by many to be the gold standard of dividend ETFs], a rotation away from growth-oriented exposure to quality, lower-vol equities could improve SMDV’s relative performance.

SMDV’s emphasis on long-term dividend growers naturally tilts the portfolio toward more defensive sectors like financials and other cash flow stable businesses. In a scenario characterized by an economic slowdown, elevated volatility, and/or risk-off sentiment, the characteristics of SMDV could become more attractive. In such a scenario, one could see SMDV outperforming its peers.

Additionally, in terms of capital preservation, SMDV’s focus on quality could prove to be attractive for investors. Under the various possible conditions I have outlined in this article, the fund’s recent relative underperformance could reverse, proving to be a risk for my current “Hold” rating assigned.

Final Takeaway

SMDV offers a unique approach to invest in small-cap dividend growers, with holdings that lean defensive. However, in the current market environment, the fund has underperformed when compared to funds with greater technology and growth exposure.

While the underlying strategy remains sound, SMDV lacks a current catalyst for outperformance. The combination of relative underperformance and less attractive valuation metrics to alternatives reduces its appeal for the allocation of new capital. For that reason, I currently assign a “Hold” rating to SMDV. This rating is further supported by Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, which also currently assigns a "Hold" rating to the fund.

Despite any significant catalysts, I acknowledge that the fund, for certain investors, may serve a role as a tactical or complementary holding (adding small-cap Russell 2000 exposure to one’s portfolio). Its low beta and defensive tilt may prove beneficial in a more risk-off environment, but until such a shift materializes, the fund is likely to remain a relative laggard.

data.title: Why I Am Upgrading REGL
data.description:
I initiated coverage of ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL) in early February as an alternative to the very popular Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF (VTI), saying that REGL was a Hold. While it is higher now than it was then, I am upgrading it to Buy today.

A Look at REGL

In that last piece, I discussed the website for REGL and the Fact Sheet published as of December 31st as good sources of information. The Fact Sheet is still as of year-end. The ETF launched more than 11 years ago, and is focused on mid-cap companies that have grown dividends in each of the past fifteen years. It is the only ETF focused on its index, the S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats.

I understand that a lot of people are pay attention ETFs and funds that are focused on dividends, but the number of followers at Seeking Alpha for REGL is only 2140. The ETF does have assets currently at $1.76 billion, which is much larger than ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV), which I really like, at $664 million with just 1720 followers.

The index that REGL tracks has 67 companies with market caps from $1.7 billion to $27.0 billion as of the end of Q1. The index is reconstituted annually, and this happened in Q1. ProShares suggests that the P/E of the ETF at quarter-end was 16.4X, with a price to book value ratio of 2.0X, but I am not confident that those are correct (and share a different P/E below). The fee is 0.4%.

The very largest position is just 1.9% of the ETF, and the top 10 make up 17.5%. So, the ETF is not highly concentrated. As of April 17th, the ETF was very exposed to four sectors, with extremely low exposure to four others:

Some may be concerned about the Financials, as large-cap Financials are down year-to-date despite their recent bounce, but I like the idea of smaller Financials, as M&A could work in their favor. These exposures are similar to SMDV's sector weightings, where Financials, Industrials and Utilities make up 70.9% of the ETF. Both ETFs have low exposure to Technology.

REGL is liquid, trading an average of 57 thousand shares per day over the past month, which works out to about $5 million per day. Seeking Alpha gives the ETF a B+ for liquidity:

The REGL Price Action

Since I initiated on REGL, it has rallied, but so has the overall market, with REGL trailing the State Street SPDR S&P MidCap 400 ETF (MDY) but exceeding the State Street SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY):

Since the end of March, REGL, at up 5.1%, has trailed SPY at +9.2% and MDY at +7.9%.

One thing I don't like about the chart for REGL is that the price gapped up on April 8th:

The ETF is still below its all-time high set in February after my article, which is in contrast to the S&P 500 and the S&P 400, both of which posted new all-time highs this week. I see resistance at 92 for REGL and support at 88.

REGL vs. Peers

I follow several divided-Focused ETFs out of the 85 that I follow closely, and REGL looks attractive compared to most of them in my view. Here are some key metrics:

Most of these are large-cap focused, while REGL and SMDV are not. REGL has returned less over the past year and the past five years than the peers, and it is one of the smaller ETFs here. The fee is higher than some, but not really that high in my view. It is not widely followed.

Going back to the end of 2019, which was before the pandemic hit, the REGL total return of 76.5% is in line with the average return of these dividend-focused ETFs:

SPY has returned 141.6% during this time-frame. while MDY has returned 91.7%. REGL, then, has lagged the overall market.

Where I Think REGL Could Go

I am concerned about stocks right now due to the slowing economy, inflation risks and the large and growing federal debt. With that said, the stocks held by REGL are higher quality than the overall market, and it has no exposure to the very large companies.

With a total return so far in 2026 of 8.5%, REGL is outpacing the S&P 500, which has returned 4.4%, but it is lagging the S&P 400, which has returned 10.6%. Since the end of 2022, REGL has returned 37.2%, while SPY has returned 93.7% and MDY has returned 56.4%. The recent move has offset only partially the strong underperformance for the three years ending in 2025.

As I think about where REGL can go in 2026, I have three scenarios that I am contemplating. First, if the S&P 500 keeps advancing, REGL will likely lag as it did from late 2022. Earnings in this scenario are likely to meet or exceed current projections. Second, if the S&P 500 falls sharply, REGL is likely to decline but fall less. In this scenario, earnings are likely to be lower. Finally, the third scenario is essentially neutral for the S&P 500, with smaller stocks continuing this recent trend of outperformance.

Before I proceed, the current portfolio, sorted inversely by size here but with the overall summary attached, has a forward P/E of 18.4X. The highest forward P/E is 44X, while the lowest has a P/E of 8X. The enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA for the ETF is 11X, with a range for its holdings of 5.4X to 27.7X. Here is the summary with the top 15 holdings of REGL:

In the S&P 500 bull scenario, I think that if the S&P 500 advances 20% for 2026, smaller stocks will likely rally but not quite as much. To get to a 20% price return, the S&P 500 will be advancing another 15.3%. If REGL ends up rallying in price by 16% for the year, it will rally another 7.6%. The total return for REGL from here will be about 9%. The P/E would be similar to what it is currently, as earnings growth would leave it about 18X despite the higher price. According to State Street, SPY currently has a P/E of 22X, so REGL would continue to have a lower P/E than the S&P 500.

My very bearish scenario for stocks may lead to a price decline, but I think that the relatively low valuation of REGL relative to the S&P 500 will cushion this decline. If the S&P were to pull back substantially, falling back to the lows of 2025, it would decline in price by 31.1% in price. REGL, if it were to pull back to its low of 2025, would decline 20.0%, but I don't expect it to fall that much. First, there is a lot more interest currently in small-caps and mid-caps. Second, the lower valuation and higher quality should help REGL. I believe that the forward P/E could drop to 15X in this scenario, and that would suggest a drop in price of 9% if earnings estimates remain the same. This would be a total return of -7.5% to the end of this year.

The neutral scenario, where the S&P 500 stays near its current level, could lead to continued appreciation of REGL as it catches up to the strong performance of the rest of the market over the past several years. If the overall stock market stays near the current level, the REGL P/E could expand to 20X, which is still below the current S&P 500 PE. Assuming the estimates remain constant, this would result in a price higher by 21.2% at year-end, giving REGL a return of about +23% from here. Notably, it will still be substantially behind the growth in MDY since the end of 2022.

Weighting these three scenarios at 20% for the bullish scenario, 30% for the bearish scenario and 50% for the neutral scenario gives an expected total return of 11%, which compares favorably to the current return on cash of less than 4%. Even if I change the weightings to 30% neutral and 50% bearish, the return works out to 5.0%, which exceeds cash.

Risks to REGL

This ETF is highly diversified, but it could fall substantially, especially if the overall stock market is under pressure. REGL has not lived through a long bear market, but it was around during the pandemic. In Q1 of 2020, REGL fell 25.2%, which was more than the SPY decline of 19.9% but less than the MDY decline of 30.0%. REGL trades currently 20.0% above its 52-week low, while the current holdings trade in a range of 3.8% to 159.5% above it with a weighted average of 32.8%. I shared a pessimistic scenario above, but the S&P 500 could fall a lot more than that scenario.

I view the M&A environment as favorable, but a risk to REGL is that the federal government curtails consolidation. Another risk is that interest rates continue to rise. While this is a problem for stocks in general, REGL has high exposure to Financials, and rising rates could be a problem for that sector.

Another risk is the war with Iran, which has caused oil to spike. REGL has very little exposure to Energy stocks. A final risk that I see is the relatively high exposure to Materials at 14.2% compared to the S&P 500 exposure of just 2%, given that the sector within the S&P 500 is up 14.9% in 2026 and could reverse. The 9 Materials names currently in REGL have returned an average of 13.7% so far. with four returning more than 20%.

Conclusion

I am somewhat bearish on stocks generally right now, but I like REGL. Despite being up year-to-date and a lot higher than its recent low, I am upgrading it from Hold to Buy. I shared three scenarios above, and why REGL stands out is that it has less downside risk and could perform very well in my neutral S&P 500 scenario. I believe that this scenario is the most likely of the three that I shared. While my analysis suggests that REGL will fall if the S&P 500 falls, that is not what happened earlier this year and may continue. Again, REGL lagged very badly from the end of 2022 through the end of 2025.

For those who can hold cash and don't have to be invested in stocks, I am not so sure that it should be bought right now, but for those who own large-cap stocks, especially large-cap Technology stocks or the very largest names, this is a great alternative.

I have SMDV rated as a Buy, which I reiterated in January and still like despite its continued advance. I actually like SMDV more than REGL, but I include both in my ETF model portfolio at both about the same size of just above 13%. REGL is appealing due to its larger size in assets and the higher market caps in its portfolio compared to SMDV's. I think that these both look a lot better than the S&P 500 currently and value their lack of excessive concentrations as well as the quality of their holdings.

data.title: AXIA: Key Infrastructure For AI, Beyond Electrical Potential
data.description:
AXIA Energia (AXIA) is a Brazilian electric company with the largest installed capacity in South America, with 44.4 GW. AXIA focuses on generation and transmission, but not distribution; therefore, it is not necessary to measure the number of users it supplies, but rather the strength of its energy infrastructure. Much of that generation and transmission power that is AXIA was explained in my previous article, where I also detailed why it was my bet on Brazil for 2026.

Brazil (EWZ), which rose almost 30% so far in 2026, is a market that has always caught my attention due to its big economic potential, as it is the largest country on the continent (after the U.S.). There are a lot of interesting sectors to invest in, but because I like the investments in energy infrastructure, I set my eyes on AXIA. However, today I want to show you the other key part of my thesis on the company, which is also connected to electric power: the opportunities I see for AXIA to become the nexus with hyperscalers to be the source of AI infrastructure in South America.

AXIA has risen 41.23% since the publication of my article on December 24, 2025, compared to 2.86% for the S&P 500 (SP500). The return occurred in a context of high volatility due to the conflict in the Middle East. Besides, the company presented very good results during Q4 2025; it maintained its profitability outlook thanks to the new fee pricing scheme, and it raised its investment expectations.

I added to this the future potential to supply AI development, and that's why I continue to give a Buy recommendation for AXIA. I understand those who invested at the end of 2025 or earlier in the company are analyzing the possibility of lowering their exposure and taking some profits. In my case, I believe there is still more potential ahead.

If a hyperscaler lands in Brazil, it will need to shake AXIA's hand

It's true there's nothing settled on commercial agreements. But in the conference call after the Q4 2025 earnings presentation, management said they are having talks with hyperscalers to supply AI data centers. The challenges are structural. In other words: they need more energy and better operational efficiency for the AI infrastructure to work more efficiently. Italo Freitas, Vice-President of AXIA, said:

We are talking to some data center players, large, major players, and the question is that today, all of the infrastructure challenge that Brazil needs to adapt in order to be able to receive this big data center, a large data center, hyperscale, more than 500 mega, or 1 gigawatt, or 1.5 gigawatts, it brings operational challenges to the system, especially in the application, if it's a training application or inference. If it's training, it may simply stop overnight and the frequency of the system may go down, and ONS is not prepared for that.

So there is a structural challenge that must be adjusted to receive these big loads. But naturally, Brazil, especially the Northeast, it's a region that will help a lot to have a large load. So we are talking to some players and looking at all of these points, the operational points that need to be adjusted.

Conference call, February 27, 2026

While I see the energy crisis in the Strait of Hormuz, I think that investing in electricity through diversified sources is the best investment. But as I said: AXIA is not just a generator, it also carries large energy loads on its transmission lines. Transmission is key to sustaining the high voltages that AI data centers generate. Therefore, if in the future a mega data center is built in Brazil, it's hard to think there won't be an agreement before with AXIA.

And this is very important because you can see it in the valuation multiples. Large utilities already have contracts with hyperscalers, as do some former cryptocurrency miners that are transitioning into AI hosting. In those companies, the contracts are translated into premium prices. On the other hand, part of my search for energy investment is based on anticipating future deals with hyperscalers. I believe AXIA, having nothing closed yet, still trades at accessible prices. Meanwhile, the recent rise is based on the other part of my investment thesis: the company's mega operational capacity.

AXIA in the transmission investment boom

Anyway, I don't think AXIA will fail if it isn't the AI's power source. I mean: if there are no agreements with hyperscalers in 2026, it doesn't change my buying thesis. And I arrived at that conclusion because electricity demand will grow due to several sectors in the coming years, as I explained in some of my recent articles, among them the one about American Electric Power (AEP). In that article I said:

"Electric companies have a long term catalyst: AI data centers that need too much energy to

work. I believe many companies in the sector have already priced in that potential. CAPEX’s projection for the workload in AI data centers is $1.3 trillion. But electricity demand growth projections will not be limited to data centers only.

The population is consuming more and more electricity due to economic growth, the replacement of gas heating, industrial use, EVs, among other factors. In the growth outlook, industrial and commercial demand will keep representing more than 50% of consumption, followed by residential consumption".

March 24, 2026

Therefore, in this global electrification boom, large generators with efficient infrastructure could become increasingly profitable. The electrification boom is not a new interpretation. But what I try to do in my investments is to look for opportunities with the greatest potential and without premium valuations that discount future profitability. So here comes AXIA, because it is a foreign alternative and operates in a volatile and politically unstable market like Brazil, it means a lower discount premium.

Therefore, what I see is that value outweighs risks. For example, in that high demand for future electrification, transmission networks need to be renewed. Most investments in the coming years will be in transmission until they equal investments in distribution by 2028. In summary, investments in transmission were 44% in 2020 and will increase to 50% in 2028. I believe that whoever takes advantage of this segment will gain more profitability.

AXIA has 74,000 km of transmission lines and represents the largest part of the investment since 2022. That investment went from R$ 1.49 billion in 2022 to R$ 4.76 billion in 2025, while another rise is expected for 2026. In the following image you will see the weight of transmission.

You can also see the breakdown of recent tenders on the right. The most recent is the ANEEL auction for the construction and maintenance of transmission lines, which adds R$1.6 billion and a RAP of R$140 million. In total, the CAPEX of the last four years is providing AXIA with an RAP of R$1.8 billion, with the predictability of regulated revenue, which ensures a return on investment. Total investments in Q4 2025 were R$3.9 billion, a 28% YoY increase. In 2025, they totaled R$9.6 billion and are expected to reach between R$12 and R$14 billion annually in 2026 and 2027.

2025 earnings: cost efficiency and focus on transmission

The best of Q4 2025, besides the long term view, I believe was the efficiency in PMSO. Those costs have kept declining since the privatization of AXIA in 2022, and I believe that is another key part of its finances. This is because as investment increases, margins could decrease in the short term. Therefore, cost control becomes crucial.

PMSO was R$ 1.69 billion in Q4 2025 compared to R$ 2.35 billion in the same period of the previous year (slide 41 of the Earnings Release). Based on what the management's said, AI is helping to lower PMSOs, but there is still plenty of room for improvement.

Revenue grew 3.1% in 2025, to R$ 41.42 billion, with strong growth in gross transmission revenue of 4.3%. Meanwhile, gross generation revenues fell by 0.8%. As I explained before, the transmission segment is the engine of investment, as well as revenue.

As a result, the company is in a transition from a more volatile business (generation) to a more stable and regulated business (transmission). The company was selling off thermal power plants last year, trying to lower costs, which in the short term meant less generation. I said generation is more volatile because it depends more on consumption. On the other hand, transmission is adjusted to regulated prices.

Both segments will remain in AXIA's portfolio, but I'm seeing this shift towards transmission as an opportunity. And this is key in my thesis, as I explained before, because if any agreement with hyperscalers isn't reached, AXIA has a predictable, stable income. But the best of all is that it improves the margins. The following image (slide 9) clearly shows the improvement in efficiency, where its regulatory EBITDA and its adjusted regulatory EBITDA grew 17.1% and 12.9% YoY, respectively.

At the same time, as you can see on the left side of the image, gross revenue dropped sharply, clearly showing that transition. The sale of assets represented R$ 997 million less in revenue due to lower generation, which was partially offset by the 9.7% increase in energy prices. But the important thing for me is margins. As you can see on slide 16, the total generation margin increased from R$ 1.47 billion in Q4 2024 to R$ 2.15 billion. This is due to improved efficiency, but also to the ACL (free contracting environment) market.

There is still potential in AXIA's shares

Despite the rally in 2026 (+40%), AXIA shares still trade at a discount compared to other major players in the utilities sector. For example, NextEra (NEE), Constellation Energy (CEG), and Southern Company (SO) are trading at EV/EBITDA FWD multiples of 15.58, 12.68, and 12.82, respectively. Meanwhile, AXIA's EV/EBITDA FWD is trading between approximately 8x and 9x. Even more: when I wrote my previous article last December, EV/EBITDA FWD was in the same range. This is explained by the projected EBITDA growth of 15.5% for AXIA.

I don't feel very comfortable giving target prices, which is why in my previous article I didn't give any target price, although I did mention discounts compared to the sector, such as the 32.2% discount on the value per book. Now, that discount is 13% (1.9x versus 2.2x). As I explained at the beginning, the rise of more than 40% so far this year could be a good moment to take some profits. I would really understand those who take that path.

But for my part, I not only feel comfortable maintaining my position, but I'm buying even more. If EV/EBITDA rises to a modest 12x or 13x, the price could reach $19 or $21, in line with the nearly 50% increase in the multiple.

Risks

In my last article I explained the risks of investing in AXIA, which were related to the political and economic context of Brazil. I mentioned the interest rates well above inflation and the presidential elections in October. I won't retell them. I'll focus on expanding on those I already mentioned. Specifically two.

First, Brazil's fiscal deficit, that is usually high. In 2025 it was around 8.3% of the GDP. During the presidency of Jair Bolsonaro, it dropped to approximately 4%. Despite the budget shortfall, I believe Lula is putting the public finances on the agenda for a possible fourth term, according to Reuters. He would do it through budget cuts and limits on tax breaks.

The other risk I consider for AXIA is weather-related, something I didn't write in my previous article. Droughts affect hydrology, and therefore, thermal power plants, which are still key to the generation segment. On slide 16, I found that the company anticipates a second half of the year where poor hydrology could impact generation revenues, especially in the third quarter. I believe we should follow this item closely, even though it doesn't change the core principles of my long-term thesis.

Conclusion

I'm taking advantage of this bargain in the utilities sector, which has already risen more than 40% so far in 2026. Is it still a bargain? I believe so for several reasons: massive operating capacity, significant investment in transmission lines, and projected stable revenues in both the regulated transmission market and the free market for generation. But is not just that. AXIA could become the key nexus for Brazil to host the AI ​​infrastructure that hyperscalers need in their data centers. I think that's a bonus that the market hasn't yet priced in because there's no public agreement in place. Finally, the EV/EBITDA FWD multiples are not only below those of the major utilities, but also below the sector average. AXIA remains my top investment in Brazil for 2026, and perhaps in all of South America within the electric utilities sector.

data.title: Managing The Cognitive Dissonance Of Long-Term Investing
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

Life entails a certain level of cognitive dissonance. It’s the discomfort you feel when you hold two conflicting beliefs at the same time - and the way your brain resolves that discomfort by rationalizing behavior.

I wrote this one day before my 50th birthday, a milestone that has me rationalizing behavior that conflicts with what I know to be true.

Belief A: Alcohol is bad for my health.

Belief B: I’m going to drink alcohol on my birthday.

The rationalization is simple. It’s for one night. What matters most to my long-term health are decisions made over decades, not over single nights.

Investing presents a similar tension, but with far higher stakes.

Belief A: Trying to time the market is bad for my investing health.

Belief B: Reducing exposure until risks subside will make me feel safer.

The rationalization many investors often reach is confidence in their own intuition. “I’ll know when to get back in. I’ll recognize the signal. I’ll avoid the worst of the decline and still participate fully in the recovery.”

The difference between these two examples is stark. In the first case, I may wake up tired and dehydrated. In the second, investors may permanently erode my long-term investment returns. The cost of resolving that discomfort is much higher.

Market movements in an uncertain world

Markets rarely reward those who wait for certainty. By the time risks feel resolved, prices have often already adjusted. For the most part, markets are forward-looking and probabilistic versus reactive and emotional.

Recent events provide a clear example. Despite elevated geopolitical risk, markets have behaved as though a worst-case outcome was unlikely. The S&P 500 Index bottomed on March 30 following a peak-to-trough decline of “only” 9.01%.1 They appeared to be looking through the conflict in the Middle East, anticipating a relatively short duration and limited economic spillovers.

So, when Iran announced on Friday that the Strait of Hormuz would remain open during the ceasefire, it felt like confirmation rather than surprise.2 Oil prices, which had been primarily falling since the end of March, plunged.3 The S&P 500 Index, which had been rallying for most of the month, closed at an all-time high.4 Small-cap and emerging market stocks extended gains that began in mid-March.5 Credit spreads tightened further, and are now lower than the days before the conflict began.6

Forward-looking versus reactive

Markets being forward-looking and probabilistic for the most part, rather than reactive and emotional, is an important distinction. Waiting on the sidelines for Friday’s announcement about the opening of the Strait of Hormuz may not have been rewarded. A lot of positive price action came before the good news and continued Friday, before cautious investors were likely able to respond to it. Weekend events resulted in the Strait being closed again, but the point still holds. While there may be near-term volatility as a result, the market may still trade as if a negotiated deal is forthcoming.

Importantly, many fundamentals appeared to reinforce this market resilience. Corporate earnings were off to a solid start, particularly among large banks.7 Jobless claims remained low.8 Regional purchasing manager surveys have been moving higher, suggesting firmer manufacturing momentum.9 Growth in the United Kingdom has exceeded expectations.10 Growth in China has also come in better than the most pessimistic forecasts.11

Many investors tend to stay invested because they accept cognitive dissonance as part of the process. They recognize that their time horizon is longer than the lifespan of any single conflict. They think in probabilities rather than certainties. Much like decisions in life, investment decisions are best judged over decades, not nights.
